title: Stranger Than Fiction
logline: An IRS auditor discovers his life is being narrated by an omniscient voice—and when he tracks down the author, he learns she's writing a story in which he dies.
antagonistOrConflict: Kay Eiffel (the author narrating his life) and the inevitability of his narrated death
tone: Whimsical, melancholic, philosophical, with moments of warmth and existential dread
themes: The value of a life lived vs. a life observed

actBreaks.description: Harold meets Professor Hilbert and begins actively investigating the voice, shifting from passive confusion to active pursuit of answers about his narrative.

validation.ruling: Scene #37 marks Harold's commitment to solving the mystery with Hilbert's structured approach via a 23-question test, fundamentally shifting from denial to active investigation of the narrator phenomenon, establishing the new direction for Act 2.

actBreaks.description: Harold discovers Kay Eiffel is his author and races to confront her, transforming the story from philosophical inquiry into urgent life-or-death stakes.

validation.ruling: Scene #90 is the irreversible turning point where Harold calls Kay and she realizes her fictional character is real and alive, shattering the boundary between author and creation, forcing both into the final act's moral crisis about whether Harold must die.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality: This script operates on dual timelines—Harold's increasingly liberated life and Kay's struggle to kill him—that converge with devastating precision. The structure is deceptively simple: a man hears a voice (scenes 1-14), investigates it (15-79), finds its source (80-100), and confronts his fate (101-134). But within this framework, Helm builds a sophisticated meditation on authorship and autonomy. The first act is metronomic, matching Harold's routinized existence with repetitive scene construction. The second act fractures this rhythm as Harold learns to live—the montage sequences (scenes 48-56, 67-73) accelerate while maintaining emotional specificity. The third act becomes a moral negotiation between creator and creation, culminating not in death but in mutual transformation.
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: The script's pacing mirrors Harold's psychological state. Early scenes are brief and mechanical (scenes 1-12 average under a page), creating claustrophobia. The Ana audit sequence (scenes 15-43) stretches time, allowing relationship development within bureaucratic constraint. The guitar-learning montage (scenes 61-73) uses rapid cuts to convey weeks of growth in pages. The final act slows dramatically—scene 93 through the climax luxuriates in conversation and consequence, giving philosophical weight its necessary breathing room. The bus stop finale (scenes 110-111) compresses catastrophe into seconds, then expands the aftermath across multiple locations simultaneously.

incitingIncident.description: Harold informs Ana Pascal, a tattooed baker in a punk-rock bakery filled with political propaganda, that he's auditing her, prompting her to curse repeatedly and accuse him of targeting her for her anti-corporate politics before he explains the audit is random and leaves her his card.

validation.ruling: Scene #3 is merely Harold's daily routine of catching the bus. Scene #15 (p12, INT. LIBERTY BAKERY) is the true inciting incident where Harold first hears the Narrator's voice describing his imminent death, shattering his ordinary world and establishing the central conflict that cannot be ignored.

midpoint.description: Harold approaches Ana at the bakery carrying a carton of flours as she's locking up, and after awkward exchanges about gifts and inconsistency, he explains he brought them because he wants her, and she invites him to dinner.

validation.ruling: Scene #73 is a romantic moment but lacks the structural weight of a midpoint. Scene #79 (p83, INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE) is the midpoint where Hilbert reveals Harold is in a tragedy and will die, forcing Harold to shift from reactive investigation to proactive action—he must find the author to save his life, raising stakes to life-or-death and creating a point of no return.

climax.description: Harold wakes up in a hospital room with injuries including a cast on his leg, arm in a sling, bandaged head, and gauze on his wrist, and a doctor tells him he was brave for stepping in front of the bus to save a boy, lists his extensive injuries, and explains his watch saved his life by deflecting the impact.

validation.ruling: Scene #110 shows Harold waking up, not the climactic confrontation. Scene #120 (p113, INT. HOSPITAL ROOM DAY) is the climax where Harold lies in a hospital bed having saved a child by jumping in front of a bus, demonstrating his character arc completion by choosing to accept his death heroically, resolving the main story question of whether he will die and how he will face it.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth 76 times in his mint-colored bathroom while a narrator describes his routine, noting his wristwatch wishes he'd use a more colorful toothbrush.

scenes.heading: INT. CLOSET -- MOMENTSLATER
scenes.summary: Harold ties his necktie in a single Windsor knot while the narrator explains this saves him 43 seconds every morning, though his watch thinks it makes his neck look fat.

scenes.heading: EXT. STREET CORNER -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold runs 57 steps per block for 6 blocks with an apple in his teeth and just catches the 8:17 Faraday Bus.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold races through his IRS office completing tax files and instantly calculating 89 times 1,417 as 126,113 for a co-worker.

scenes.heading: INT. WAREHOUSE -- DAY
scenes.summary: Harold eats lunch alone at a steel desk in a warehouse, finishing his last bite precisely when his watch beeps after 45.7 minutes.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold walks home alone past urban shops, pausing momentarily at a window displaying guitars.

scenes.heading: INT. KITCHEN -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold eats a modest meal alone in his kitchen while looking through a calculator catalog.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold goes to bed at 11:13, placing his wristwatch on the nightstand and turning off the light, as the narrator announces that Wednesday his watch changed everything.
scenes.turningPointNote: The narrator foreshadows that Wednesday will break Harold's routine and change his life.

scenes.heading: INT. NIGHTSTAND -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold's wristwatch displays 7:15am and begins beeping.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth but suddenly stops when he hears the narrator's voice, pauses to listen, then resumes brushing only to stop again when the narration stops abruptly with him.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold first becomes aware of the narrator's voice describing his life, establishing the central conflict.

scenes.heading: INT. CLOSET -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold tries to tie his tie while the narrator speaks, but he keeps stopping to search his apartment for the source of the voice, looking over his shoulders and closing his eyes before reluctantly resuming.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold runs for the bus with a pear in his mouth while the narrator describes this as his last morning doing these things, causing Harold to stop and wiggle his squeaking shoes in amazement before missing the bus and throwing his arms up in disgust.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- DAY
scenes.summary: Harold stumbles distractedly through the IRS office, nearly knocking over a female co-worker, unable to concentrate on his work or calculate arithmetic he normally does effortlessly.

scenes.heading: INT. ARCHIVES -- DAY
scenes.summary: Harold stands frozen in the archives staring at the water cooler when his co-worker Dave asks if he's okay, and Harold tells Dave he's being followed, explaining he hears a voice narrating his life that knows impossibly specific details.

scenes.heading: INT. LIBERTY BAKERY -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold informs Ana Pascal, a tattooed baker in a punk-rock bakery filled with political propaganda, that he's auditing her, prompting her to curse repeatedly and accuse him of targeting her for her anti-corporate politics before he explains the audit is random and leaves her his card.

scenes.heading: EXT. LIBERTY BAKERY -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold walks outside the bakery trying to calm himself when the narrator describes him as beleaguered, causing Harold to scream at the heavens to shut up and leave him alone.

scenes.heading: EXT. ROOFTOP OP BUILDING DAY
scenes.summary: A worn, yellowish woman named Kay in oversized clothes approaches the ledge of a skyscraper, smokes a cigarette, and steps up onto the ledge looking down at the city below.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Kay stands on a factory table in her loft when Penny Escher arrives introducing herself as the assistant hired by publishers, and Kay reveals Penny is there as a watchdog to ensure she finishes her book after ten years, admitting she hasn't written anything in months and doesn't know how her main character dies yet.
scenes.turningPointNote: Kay is revealed as the author writing Harold's story, establishing the meta-narrative structure and her struggle to finish the book that will determine Harold's fate.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE-~ 'AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold arrives at his desk to find a note from Dr. Cayly asking to chat, and he sighs.

scenes.heading: INT. HUMAN RESOURCES OFFICES -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold passes children building a Lego tower outside Dr. Cayly's office and tells them they're going to have an extra red block.

scenes.heading: INT. CAYLY'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold sits in Dr. Cayly's office as the therapist uses words like "wibbly-wobbly" and "convo," prompting the narrator to call Cayly an idiot who uses corporate platitudes, which causes Harold to look up and realize the voice is still narrating.

scenes.heading: INT. HUMANRESOURCES OFFICES -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold leaves the Human Resources office as the camera reveals the children have built Lego buildings with one extra red block in the middle and a Lego man in a black suit who gets run over by a Lego bus.

scenes.heading: EXT. BUS STOP -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold stands exhausted at a bus stop when his watch begins beeping and flashing loudly as Ana walks up the street across from him, but Harold doesn't notice her and shakes his watch trying to stop the beeping.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold bursts into his living room, throws down his briefcase and coat, nearly trips over his ottoman, and demands to know where the voice is.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold grabs his toothbrush and begins brushing his teeth, pausing with each move hoping the voice will return, then yells that he heard it say his actions would result in his death before throwing down his toothbrush and storming out.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold frantically grabs ties, lamps, and pillows trying to make the narrator speak, narrating his own actions aloud and begging the voice to say something before collapsing against his bed in defeat.

scenes.heading: INT. THERAPIST'S OFFICE -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold sits uncomfortably in therapist Dr. Mittag-Leffler's office explaining he hears a voice narrating what he's already done, not telling him what to do, and insists he's a character in someone's story, but she diagnoses him with schizophrenia.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold searches online in his cubicle and finds a web page for Professor Jules Hilbert, a Literary Dean Emeritus who teaches a course called "The Entire History of Everything Literate," and his fingers stop on the mouse.

scenes.heading: EXT. URBANUNIVERSITY CAMPUS -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold consults a piece of yellow paper trying to navigate the urban university campus and stops a passing student to ask for directions.

scenes.heading: INT. ERDOSOFFICES -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert leads Harold down a dark corridor at a clipped pace, confirming Harold called about the narrator that says he's going to die and asking how long he has to live.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hilbert offers Harold a seat in his book-filled office, asks if he's crazy, then rapid-fires questions about whether Harold counted hallway tiles, his job at the IRS, living alone, having pets, and his hobbies, before explaining Harold is either in a comedy or tragedy and they need to determine which.
scenes.turningPointNote: Hilbert establishes the framework for understanding Harold's situation as a literary narrative, setting up the central question of whether Harold is in a comedy or tragedy.

scenes.heading: INT. BUS -- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold rides the bus transcribing the narrator's words on a legal pad when Ana Pascal boards, sees him, and turns to walk away, but Harold calls out to her and she reluctantly sits down to discuss her audit before getting off at her stop.

scenes.heading: EXT. OUTSIDE BUS -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold steps off the bus feeling elated about his encounter with Ana when the narrator reveals he exited 27 blocks too early and will have to walk, causing Harold to curse and ask why the voice couldn't tell him earlier.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth still counting strokes but less emphatically while his wristwatch looks on from the nightstand.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold crawls into bed, removes his wristwatch, places it on the nightstand, and presses buttons to set its alarm.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold runs toward the bus stop with a peach in his teeth, counting his steps and arriving with plenty of time as his watch smugly peers up at him.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert lets Harold into his office on a rainy morning and explains he's devised a 23-question test to uncover truths about the narrator, asking Harold to be candid as he holds out his glasses.

scenes.heading: EXT. INTERSECTION -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: An SUV with Kay inside spins out of control across a rainy intersection, hops the curb heading toward a shoe store, and crashes violently through the display window with Kay thrusting through the windshield as customers and employees dive out of the way.

scenes.heading: EXT. CORNER ACROSS FROM SHOE STORE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Kay sits on a bus stop bench across from the intact shoe store with Penny holding an umbrella, explaining they're imagining car wrecks and discussing that 41% of accidents occur in inclement weather before disposing of her cigarette.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hilbert finishes his quiz with Harold, asking about his belief system (math), favorite word (integer), and aspirations, with Harold revealing he's always wanted to learn guitar, prompting Hilbert to tell him to go do it since he might die soon.
scenes.turningPointNote: Hilbert advises Harold to pursue what he's always wanted to do, catalyzing Harold's transformation from passive routine to active living.

scenes.heading: INT. LIBERTY BAKERY-- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold arrives at the bakery early with his briefcase, pad, and pen, waves at Ana who doesn't wave back, makes marks in his pad as they exchange greetings, and asks to see her files.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Ana presents Harold with a large box of loose papers as her tax files, sarcastically claiming she put them in the box just to mess with him, and a montage begins of Harold working through the disorganized files.

scenes.heading: INT. KITCHEN -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold emerges exhausted from the office after everyone has gone and watches Ana baking cookies in the kitchen, and when she offers him one he declines saying he doesn't like cookies, prompting her to ask what's wrong with him before insisting he try one, which he admits is the best cookie he's ever tasted.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold accepts Ana's cookie and admits it's delicious, marking a shift in their relationship and his willingness to break from rigid routine.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold walks down the city sidewalk at night as the narrator describes how fate must work with remarkable precision to cause catastrophic events, and Harold sits at a bus stop looking at his watch.

scenes.heading: INT. LOCKER ROOM -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert in a Speedo and swim cap tells Harold not to get on any trains and asks about Carla and the boy on the bicycle, suggesting they're other characters in Harold's story.

scenes.heading: INT. SWIMMINGPOOL -- CONTINOOUS
scenes.summary: Hilbert tells Harold not to do anything tomorrow—stay home, don't answer the phone, don't brush his teeth, don't go to work—to avoid moving the plot forward and see if the plot finds him instead, then puts on his nose plug and dives into the pool.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold turns down his sheets about to climb into bed but stops before taking his watch off.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold transforms the area around his couch into a makeshift campground with a cooler, bowl of water, soap, towel, and snacks, tosses down a pillow, and takes his watch off.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM -- MORNING
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM MID-MORNING
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM-- NOON
scenes.summary: Harold sits on his couch eating popcorn and watching TV when the phone rings repeatedly, but he only stares at it without answering.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM-- MID-AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold lies nearly comatose on the couch as the TV announces three more hours of Mr. Belvedere, and the phone rings again while he sinks his head into his hands.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM-- LATE AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold watches TV as the sun sets, mail comes through the door, the phone rings, his watch beeps, and he suddenly notices a large creaking sound and shadow descending over his apartment.

scenes.heading: EXT. STREET -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: A construction foreman and crew look up to see Harold yelling from the third story of a partially demolished brownstone, and the foreman orders them to stop the crane as Harold protests he was watching TV.

scenes.heading: INT. CLASSROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold barges into Professor Hilbert's classroom carrying two suitcases and explains that a wrecking ball came into his apartment while he was doing nothing, and Hilbert confirms this is plot, not coincidence.

scenes.heading: INT. HALLWAY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert tells Harold he doesn't control his fate but offers to analyze Harold's journal to identify the author through sentence structure, warning Harold to live his life while Hilbert works because the narrator might kill him.
scenes.turningPointNote: Hilbert shifts from trying to determine genre to accepting Harold's death is likely and urging him to live his remaining time fully — a major pivot in Harold's approach to his situation.

scenes.heading: EXT. ACROSS THE STREET FROM LIBERTY BAKERY -- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold stands across the street from the bakery carrying his suitcases, watching Ana inside as she serves a homeless man, then looks up for guidance from the voice but receives none and walks away.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S GUEST BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Dave shows Harold the guest bedroom filled with Battlestar Galactica memorabilia and life-sized cardboard cutouts, and when Dave asks how long Harold is staying, Harold pauses and says he doesn't know.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S LIVING ROOM
scenes.summary: Harold asks Dave what he would do if he knew he was going to die, and Dave responds with absurd hypothetical questions about being rich or having superpowers before finally suggesting Harold should go to Space Camp.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S BATHROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth with precise counting, then stops, thinks, and suddenly begins brushing with random varied strokes before looking at himself invigorated and saying 'Space Camp.'

scenes.heading: INT. MUSIC STORE -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold stands in a music store staring at guitars as the Narrator describes his oldest desire, and he picks up and rejects several guitars including a black Les Paul and a red Stratocaster before selecting a Fender.

scenes.heading: INT. SHEET MUSIC DEPARTMENT -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold asks a sales representative for a book to learn guitar, and after confused exchanges about what kind of guitar Harold wants to play, the representative directs him to beginner guitar books.

scenes.heading: INT. HOSPITAL EME~GENCY ROOM -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Kay and Penny sit in a hospital emergency room observing patients, with Kay dismissing various injuries as non-fatal until a young man on a gurney is rushed past screaming in agony.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S LIVING ROOM-- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold sits on Dave's couch practicing guitar with a tablature book, humming along to his simple playing as the Narrator says he felt more at peace with every awkward strum despite his approaching demise.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S DINING ROOM TABLE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold and Dave sit at the dining table eating sandwiches and laughing together as the Narrator says Harold no longer ate alone.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S BATHROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth without counting, bobbing his head to an imaginary beat as the Narrator says he no longer counted brush strokes.

scenes.heading: EXT. DAVE'S- FIRE ESCAPE -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Harold sits on Dave's fire escape eating cereal and looking at the city while Dave gets dressed for work, as the Narrator says Harold no longer worried about the time it took to put on his tie.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold strolls past the bus stop toward a park, smiling at stiff commuters as the Narrator says he no longer counted his steps to the bus stop.

scenes.heading: INT. MOVIE THEATER -- DAY
scenes.summary: Harold sits alone in a movie theater watching Manhattan, eating popcorn and laughing heartily as the Narrator says Harold did what had terrified him before.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold walks past the bus stop watching exhausted commuters exit the bus and tries to hide his glee as the Narrator says Harold Crick lived his life.

scenes.heading: INT. DAVE'S LIVING ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold sits on the couch strumming guitar with more confidence and singing with vigor as the Narrator says with every strum he became stronger in who he was and why he was alive.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold crawls into bed, removes his wristwatch, and turns off the light, but his watch begins to glow and casts light on a file labeled 'Audit: Karen Eiffel' on the nightstand.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold discovers the identity of his narrator through the glowing watch illuminating Karen Eiffel's audit file — the key information that will allow him to confront his author.

scenes.heading: EXT. LIBERTY BAKERY -- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold approaches Ana at the bakery carrying a carton of flours as she's locking up, and after awkward exchanges about gifts and inconsistency, he explains he brought them because he wants her, and she invites him to dinner.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold and Ana move from antagonistic auditor-taxpayer relationship to romantic connection when she invites him to dinner — a major shift in their dynamic and Harold's emotional journey.

scenes.heading: EXT. RESIDENTIAL AREA -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold and Ana get off a bus and walk down the street, and when Ana stops and invites Harold up to her place for dinner, he hesitates before she tells him to come up and he agrees.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S APARTMENT -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold and Ana finish sushi at her apartment, and after conversation about his job, Harold notices an acoustic guitar on the couch and tells Ana he only knows one song, then plays and sings 'Whole Wide World' for her.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold and Ana are in her dark bedroom with decidedly sexual sounds, and Harold pauses to say 'Wow' and tells Ana he's not counting.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold lies asleep in bed next to Ana as the Narrator says Harold's life was filled with significant and mundane moments that remained indistinguishable to him, until Harold opens his eyes.

scenes.heading: EXT. MARKET -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Kay exits a market with cigarettes and tissues, and when a worker spills apples that roll into the street, Kay stops before lighting her cigarette and watches an apple roll toward traffic.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold bursts into Hilbert's office announcing it's a comedy because Ana has fallen in love with him and the voice confirmed it, and Hilbert shows Harold a list of seven living authors who could be his narrator, but Harold insists it's a comedy and refuses to read the list.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Kay enters the loft carrying yellow sheets and tells Penny she figured out how to kill Harold in a way that's simple, ironic, and possibly heartbreaking, but doesn't reveal what it is.

scenes.heading: INT. EDISON PRESS -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold rushes into Edison Press and asks the receptionist to speak with Karen Eiffel urgently, but the receptionist says she's not there and they don't have her contact information.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold stands on the sidewalk outside the publisher trying to figure out what to do next, then suddenly gets an idea and takes off running.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold rushes into his office past colleagues, ignores a co-worker saying the phones are out, boots up his computer, types frantically searching for something, then writes information on a post-it.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE (MAIN COMPUTER)-- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold scans a large computer screen, clicks, and a printer prints a document containing Karen Eiffel's name, which Harold grabs impatiently before taking off down the corridor.

scenes.heading: INT. ARCHIVES -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold rifles through archive files using his printout, finds a specific file, yanks out a page from it, and returns the file.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold tries to use a phone but discovers the phones are out, asks Dave for a cell phone but there's no reception, then asks Dave for change.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold runs down the sidewalk with a sheet and loose change searching for a pay phone, spots one on a corner, runs across the street nearly getting hit by a taxi and dropping coins, but just as he approaches the phone a businessman picks it up.

scenes.heading: EXT. SIDEWALK-- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold weaves frantically between pedestrians on the sidewalk, disregarding courtesy and the natural flow of foot traffic.

scenes.heading: INT. SUBWAY TERMINAL-- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold rushes to a bank of phones in a subway terminal, but the first has no dial tone, the second is covered in phlegm, and the third is broken, so he picks up the fourth phone which works and he dials.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Kay sits at her desk writing on her laptop and types 'The phone rang' which causes the actual phone to ring, and she experiments by typing the phrase multiple times with the phone ringing each time, then slowly types 'The phone rang a final time' and picks up the receiver.
scenes.turningPointNote: Kay discovers she has a real connection to Harold when her typed words cause the phone to ring in reality — the moment she realizes Harold Crick is not fictional.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- LATER
scenes.summary: Kay sits petrified in the loft when there's a buzz at the door, and Penny lets Harold in, who introduces himself as Kay's main character, and Kay covers her mouth in shock as he approaches.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold and Kay meet face-to-face for the first time — the protagonist confronting his author in person, confirming his existence and her power over his fate.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold and Kay sit across from each other as Harold explains he found her through an old audit file and could hear her voice narrating his life, and Kay reveals she's already written his death but hasn't typed it yet, offering to give him the manuscript.
scenes.turningPointNote: Kay reveals Harold's death is already written but not yet typed, and offers him the manuscript — the moment Harold learns his fate is sealed but not yet executed.

scenes.heading: INT. SWIMMING POOL -- DAY
scenes.summary: Harold rushes into the swimming pool with Kay's manuscript and tells Professor Hilbert he may already be dead, just not typed yet, then asks Hilbert to read it because Harold couldn't bring himself to.

scenes.heading: INT. SMALL OFFICE -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: A middle-aged black woman signs documents across from a man in a blue uniform, finishes, shakes his hand, and he hands her a blue uniform in plastic wrapping.

scenes.heading: INT. BOY'S ·BEDROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: A father hands his blonde son a brand new bicycle helmet, fastens the strap around the boy's chin, adjusts it on his head, and reassures him it'll be fine.

scenes.heading: INT. PROFESSOR HILBERT'S DINING ROOM-- EVENING
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert places the manuscript on his dining room table, sits down, thoroughly cleans his glasses, then stands and begins to read.

scenes.heading: INT. LIBERTY BAK~RY -- EVENING
scenes.summary: Harold enters the bakery looking exhausted and distraught, tells Ana something happened but can't explain it, and when she brings him cookies and milk he begins to tell her about being an auditor for nine years.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold lies asleep in bed next to Ana as the camera descends and the Narrator describes his life, and when Harold opens his eyes they are filled with tears.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- MORNING
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. BUS -- AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Harold sits on a bus reading Kay's manuscript, chuckling at parts as the bus travels through the city and the sun sets.

scenes.heading: EXT. GARMENT LOFT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold gets off the bus and approaches Kay on the street, telling her he finished reading her manuscript and found it lovely, particularly the part about guitars.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Harold enters his apartment, glances at the plastic covering his front wall, chuckles, and heads toward the bedroom.

scenes.heading: INT. BEDROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold pulls his nicest suit and orange tie from his closet.

scenes.heading: INT. LIVING ROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold makes a phone call asking about an age limit for a program and smiles when told there isn't one.

scenes.heading: EXT. BUS STOP -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold waits at a bus stop holding his suit and an overnight bag.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S APARTMENT -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold holds Ana in his arms while they watch television together, with Ana laughing at something on screen while Harold remains understandably distant.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Harold tells Ana he adores her, she says it back, then he begins explaining how to subtract the value of a foreclosure from taxable income while his watch looks on despondently.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY -- MORNING
scenes.summary: The sun rises over the city as traffic moves, street cleaners work, joggers pass each other, and buses leave terminals.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold sits at the edge of Ana's bed staring out the window as his watch changes to 7:15 and begins beeping, which he quickly turns off before Ana rustles.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold's final morning begins — the day of his prophesied death arrives

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BATHROOM-- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold brushes his teeth without paying attention while the narrator notes he does so in vain, and Harold looks up with a coy smile.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM-- MOMENTSLATER
scenes.summary: Harold ties his tie while Ana sleeps.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S APARTMENT -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold eats cereal while reading the morning paper.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold retrieves his watch from the nightstand, puts it on, and gives Ana a tender kiss on the cheek before saying goodbye.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold walks calmly down the sidewalk, taking in the city air as much as he can.

scenes.heading: EXT. BUS STOP MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Harold stands at the bus stop with other commuters including the Polish woman, while the narrator explains that Harold's watch has been three minutes ahead of schedule due to receiving incorrect time two weeks prior.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Kay sits at her desk crying and trembling as she types, pulls out a cigarette butt from tissue paper, tries unsuccessfully to light it with shaking hands, then stops and crumples the cigarette.

scenes.heading: EXT. BUS STOP -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: A middle-aged black bus driver named Carla steps off the bus incredibly upset and is comforted by someone saying it's not her fault, a blonde boy with a scraped elbow is led to the curb, and people surround Harold who has not moved.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold is struck by the bus — the prophesied death event occurs

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert stares out his window when Kay Eiffel enters his office holding the manuscript and introduces herself.

scenes.heading: INT. HOSPITAL ROOM DAY
scenes.summary: Harold wakes up in a hospital room with injuries including a cast on his leg, arm in a sling, bandaged head, and gauze on his wrist, and a doctor tells him he was brave for stepping in front of the bus to save a boy, lists his extensive injuries, and explains his watch saved his life by deflecting the impact.
scenes.turningPointNote: Harold survives his prophesied death — the watch deflects the fatal impact

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Professor Hilbert finishes reading Kay's manuscript and tells her it's okay but not great, and Kay says she's fine with okay and will rewrite the rest of the book because it's a book about a man who knows he's going to die and finds meaning in that, which wouldn't work if he simply died.
scenes.turningPointNote: Kay reveals she changed Harold's ending and her artistic reasoning for doing so

scenes.heading: INT. HOSPITAL ROOM-- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Harold takes a bite of Bavarian Sugar Cookie while Ana strokes his face and shoulders.

scenes.heading: INT. GARMENT LOFT
scenes.summary: Penny places a pack of nicotine patches on Kay's laptop.

scenes.heading: INT. OFFICE
scenes.summary: Dave opens a manila envelope and pulls out a Space Camp application.

scenes.heading: INT. BOY'S BEDROOM
scenes.summary: A father hugs his son tightly.

scenes.heading: INT. BUS TERMINAL
scenes.summary: Carla is surrounded and consoled by co-workers at the bus terminal.

scenes.heading: INT. HOSPITAL &MERGENCY ROOM-- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows Kay in the emergency room as a young gunshot victim is carried in.

scenes.heading: INT. SWIMMING POOL -- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows Hilbert at the swimming pool putting his nose plugs in.

scenes.heading: INT. LIBERTY BAKERY -- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows the homeless man eating his Danish in the bakery.

scenes.heading: INT. ANA'S BEDROOM -- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows Harold and Ana looking at each other with their heads on their pillows.

scenes.heading: INT. MUSIC STORE -- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows Harold in the music store.

scenes.heading: INT. BUS -- FLASHBACK
scenes.summary: A flashback shows Harold reading the manuscript on the bus.

scenes.heading: INT. HOSPITAL ROOM -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Ana draws anarchist symbols and propaganda on Harold's cast with a pen while he chuckles, then she affectionately touches the gauze on his wrist.

scenes.heading: INT. HILBERT'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: The camera pans down the final page of the manuscript to the words "The End."
